title: In vitro transcription of mRNA with the NEB HiScribe™ SP6 Quick RNA Synthesis Kit and subsequent enzymatic capping
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"1a24o","text":"This a protocol for in vitro transcription (IVT) of mRNA with SP6 RNA polymerase (with or without template-based polyA-tail) followed by enzymatic capping. A single reaction should produce between 60 and 160 µg mRNA. The protocol was used to produce mRNA of different fluorescent proteins for electroporation of Aiptasia (Exaiptasia diaphana) zygotes. There, a simple cap0 5′ structure and a polyA tail of 50 nt proved most efficient for expression in vivo, this might be different in other organisms. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":20,"length":8},{"style":"italic","offset":322,"length":19},{"style":"italic","offset":449,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"d718d","text":"This is a protocol spanning two days with an overnight in vitro transcription and should be followed in one go, delays or freeze-thaw cycles will lead to an increased amount of RNA degradation (independently from RNases).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":221},{"style":"italic","offset":54,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3c28t","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7b0td","text":"The RNA polymerases commonly used in in vitro transcriptions require a linear dsDNA template (otherwise they will just continue transcribing whatever lies downstream of the terminator/polyA sequence).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4iito","text":"Such a template can be produced in two ways:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ee3u3","text":"Linearizing a plasmid with a blunt-end cutting restriction enzyme (or one producing 5′ overhangs) as close as possible to the end of the SV40 polyA signal.","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"894u8","text":"Amplifying the template by PCR.","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ea03o","text":"This protocol uses PCR-generated template: it is more efficient and scalable in producing large amounts of template without requiring massive plasmid-prep endeavors and it allows easy addition of polyA tails via the reverse PCR primer, a detailed protocol can be found here: Template production for mRNA in vitro transcription by PCR","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":275,"length":58}],"data":{}},{"key":"fr49d","text":"The length of the mRNA depends on the reverse PCR primer used for template generation (or the enzyme site used for restriction), the first transcribed nucleotides is the last G in the SP6 promoter, the last one the physical end of the template, this is important for calculating the correct molar concentrations of the mRNA after purification.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fvn7f","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"906d7","text":"This protocol uses production of mNeonGreen (mNG) mRNA as an example for all steps (template amplified from pCR182 - Addgene # 240642). ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5noel","text":"We use a NEB HiScribe SP6 Quick RNA Synthesis Kit for in vitro transcription, precipipate the resulting RNA with LiCl and then add the cap0 5′ structure with the NEB Vaccinia Capping System (instructions for optional cap1 modification included). The resulting capped mRNA should be purified, QC'ed and quantified, single-use aliquoted and stored at −80°C immediately following this protocol. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":9,"length":40},{"style":"bold","offset":162,"length":27},{"style":"italic","offset":54,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"5A4D97AACB994A7C873599C1E8A8EC46","url":"http://dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.bp2l6yo4zvqe/v1"}}}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"4859j","text":"Thaw the following components of the HiScribe SP6 kit just before starting:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":75}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fumt1","text":"10 µL 10× SP6 reaction buffer","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c1m7n","text":"2.5 µL ATP (Tris)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cpph5","text":"2.5 µL GTP (Tris)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"39ad2","text":"2.5 µL UTP (Tris)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dk9fe","text":"2.5 µL CTP (Tris)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"73j13","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7u3g2","text":"If the nuclease-free water is stored at −20°C, thaw an aliquot as well. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"65tlb","text":"Use only RNase-free reagents and consumables and follow general rules for the work with RNA such as use of regularly changed gloves, treatment of any surfaces touched during the extraction with RNase decontamination solution (e.g., RNase Away or RNaseZAP) and keeping the work area clean and tidy. NEB has a nice guide here: https://www.neb.com/en-us/tools-and-resources/usage-guidelines/avoiding-ribonuclease-contamination","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":325,"length":98}],"data":{}},{"key":"rcav","text":"Keep the RNA on ice unless otherwise noted, once final capped mRNA purified and eluted into nuclease-free water, keep it on ice and finish quality control, quantification and make aliquots in one go to store the aliquots at −80°C. Having the RNA out on ice longer than necessary increases the risk of degradation. Degradation will also occur from freeze-thaw cycles (hence the single-use aliquots).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"2C08B643423211F0AB870A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://www.neb.com/en-us/tools-and-resources/usage-guidelines/avoiding-ribonuclease-contamination"}}}}

steps: Set up a 25-µL in vitro transcription reaction in the following order in a nuclease-free PCR tube at RT: x µL nuclease-free water (depending on the volume of template needed) 0.6 µL RNase inhibitor, murine 10 µL 10× SP6 reaction buffer 2.5 µL ATP (Tris) 2.5 µL GTP (Tris) 2.5 µL UTP (Tris) 2.5 µL CTP (Tris) x µL DNA template (should be 100-500 ng total) 2.5 µL SP6 polymerase mix Example assuming that the concentration of the purified PCR is 200 ng/µL: 6.9 µL nuclease-free water 0.6 µL RNase inhibitor, murine 2.5 µL 10× SP6 reaction buffer 2.5 µL ATP (Tris) 2.5 µL GTP (Tris) 2.5 µL UTP (Tris) 2.5 µL CTP (Tris) 2.5 µL DNA template (500 ng total) 2.5 µL SP6 polymerase mix
Dilute the RNA with ice-cold nuclease-free water to 2 µg/µL (if original concentration is higher). Keep on ice.

Purify the mRNA with one of the two accompanying protocols for the Zymo Research RNA Clean & Concentrator™-25 Kit (for a single 50-µg capping reaction) or Zymo Research Quick-RNA Miniprep Kit (for two pooled 50-µg capping reactions).
Incubate at 37°C for 12-16 h (overnight).
Transfer 1 µL of the mix into a new PCR tube and prepare for electrophoresis, keep on ice. This is to check on RNA integrity and size.
Add 1.5 µL of the RNA solution to the water, mix by pipetting and put on ice.
Add 1.5 µL nuclease-free water into a pre-chilled RNase-free PCR tube on ice.

Use the prepared sample to check RNA size and integrity by gel electrophoresis. We usually use either agarose in %TAE+bleach gels and standard DNA ladders and loading dyes (allows to see the degradation and get a rough estimate of size) or for a better size assessment (e.g., when a new construct has been transcribed the first time) use standard 1% agarose in TAE gels but in combination with denaturing RNA loading dye and an RNA ladder in the same dye. RNA agarose electrophoresis with bleach gels RNA agarose electrophoresis with denaturing RNA sample buffer

From the remaining mix, use 1 µL to quantify the RNA on a NanoDrop device blanked with nuclease-free water. There is no additional dilution necessary, the NanoDrop has an upper detection limit of ssRNA of >10 µg/µL.

Calculate the RNA concentration (double the measured value to account for the 1:2 dilution). The concentration should be >1.5 µg/µL for a single 25-µL IVT reaction. A 260 /A 280 and A 260 /A 230 ratios don't matter at this time.
50 µg of the RNA are used per capping reaction. For capping more than 50 µg, set up multiple reactions in parallel instead of increasing the volume.
Prepare fresh 4 mM SAM: add 1.75 µL SAM (32 mM stock from kit) to 12.25 µl nuclease-free water. Keep on ice.
Dilute and denature RNA:
In an RNase-free PCR tube, mix 25 µL RNA (50 µg total) and 50 µL nuclease-free water. If generation of cap1 structures is desired, only add 45 µL of nuclease-free water and see note on go to step #27
Place the tube in an PCR cycler and incubate 10 min at 65°C, then cool down immediately to 4°C.

Add the remaining reagents in the following order at RT (100 µL final volume): 2.5 µL RNase inhibitor, murine 10 µL 10× Capping buffer 5 µL GTP (10 mM) 2.5 µL freshly prepared SAM (4 mM) 5 µL Vaccinia capping enzyme mix (10 U/µL) For generation of cap1 structures: 2.5 µL RNase inhibitor, murine 10 µL 10× Capping buffer 5 µL GTP (10 mM) 2.5 µL freshly prepared SAM (4 mM) 5 µL Vaccinia capping enzyme mix (10 U/µL) 5 µL mRNA Cap 2´-O-Methyltransferase (50 U/μl)
Incubate in a PCR cycler at 37°C for 60 min Increase incubation time to 2 h for mRNA smaller than 200 nt.

Let the pellet dissolve for 5 min at RT with occasional mixing, once the pellet has completely dissolved, mix once more, spin down briefly and place on ice until proceeding to the next step. Flicking against the bottom of the tube is enough, make sure that the liquid always collects at the bottom where the pellet is.
Add 50 µL nuclease-free water (at RT) and close the tube.

Dry the pellet in the open tube for 2-3 min at RT. Best to have some RNase-free (wipe down with RNaseAWAY or a similar reagent) loose cover to prevent dust and contaminants entering but allowing air exchange for drying.

Remove ethanol completely by slowly pipetting with a P200 pipette. A trick for removing the liquid in one go is to take a P1000 and stack a 200-µL tip to the 1000-µL one. The goal here is to remove absolutely any liquid, a slow but continuous uptake helps to not leave any drops on the walls of the tube.
Spin for 30 s at ≥16,000 ×g at 4°C.
Carefully remove supernatant with a micropipette and add 500 µL ice-cold, RNase-free 70% ethanol. Do not touch the pellet.
Spin for 30 s at ≥16,000 ×g at 4°C.
Carefully remove supernatant with a micropipette and add 500 µL ice-cold, RNase-free 70% ethanol. Do not touch the pellet.
Centrifuge at ≥16,000 ×g for 20 min at 4°C. There should be a visible pellet afterwards.
Incubate >30 min at –20°C.
Add 25 µL (0.5 volume) RNase-free LiCl solution from kit (7.5 M LiCl, 10 mM EDTA) and mix well.
Transfer to RNase-free 1.5 mL microcentrifuge tube and place on ice.
Incubate for 15-30 min at 37°C.
Add 25 µL nuclease-free water and 2 µL DNase I to the reactions and mix well.
Transfer into an RNase-free 1.5-mL microcentrifuge tube and place on ice until proceeding with purification. The purification step should follow within 10-30 min to ensure highest RNA quality.
creator: Christian CR Renicke
